1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface $S$ over a field --- such as $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$
28	or the product $C \times C$ of a smooth projective curve $C$ with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension $2$. To extend a rational map
30	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from $S$ to an abelian variety across a codimension-$2$ closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} $2$, hence a rational function with no codimension-$1$ poles on a neighbourhood of $x$
33	extends across $x$. The depth $\geq 2$ inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring $R$ one has $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$,
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension $d$ admits an $R$-regular sequence of length $d$ generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$, i.e.\ $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its $\text{Ext}$ reformulation
47	    (Definition~\ref{def:depth} and Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~\ref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~\ref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.

61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	  [Depth of a module]
66	  \label{def:depth}
67	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
68	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
69	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
70	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
71	  % "Let $R$ be a ring, and $I \subset R$ an ideal. Let $M$ be a finite $R$-module.
72	  %  The {\it $I$-depth} of $M$, denoted $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is defined as follows:
73	  %  \begin{enumerate}
74	  %  \item if $IM \not = M$, then $\text{depth}_I(M)$ is the supremum in
75	  %  $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences in $I$,
76	  %  \item if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$.
77	  %  \end{enumerate}
78	  %  If $(R, \mathfrak m)$ is local we call $\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)$ simply
79	  %  the {\it depth} of $M$."
80	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
81	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring, let $I \subseteq R$ be an ideal, and let $M$ be a finitely
82	  generated $R$-module. The \emph{$I$-depth} of $M$, written $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is the
83	  supremum in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences contained
84	  in $I$, provided $IM \neq M$; if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$. When
85	  $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
86	  $\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)$ and call this simply the
87	  \emph{depth} of $M$. The depth of the ring $R$ itself is $\text{depth}(R)$, the depth
88	  of $R$ as a module over itself.
89	\end{definition}
90	
91	Recall that a sequence $f_1, \dots, f_r \in R$ is called \emph{$M$-regular} if
92	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0$ and for each $i$, the element $f_i$ is a non-zero-divisor on
93	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M$. The empty sequence is regular on $M$ iff $M \neq 0$ (it is
94	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention $\text{depth}(0) = \infty$
95	above is the usual one).
96	
97	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
98	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the $\text{Ext}$ characterisation (used to establish an
99	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	  [Depth via $\text{Ext}$]
103	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
104	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
105	  \uses{def:depth}
106	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
107	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
108	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
109	  % "Let $R$ be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$.
110	  %  Let $M$ be a nonzero finite $R$-module. Then $\text{depth}(M)$
111	  %  is equal to the smallest integer $i$ such that
112	  %  $\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)$ is nonzero."
113	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
114	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
115	  $\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}$ and let $M$ be a nonzero finitely generated $R$-module. Then
116	  \[
117	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
118	  \]
119	\end{lemma}
120	
121	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
122	% "Let $\delta(M)$ denote the depth of $M$ and let $i(M)$ denote
123	%  the smallest integer $i$ such that $\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)$
124	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that $i(M) < \infty$.
125	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-ideal-nonzerodivisor} we have
126	%  $\delta(M) = 0$ if and only if $i(M) = 0$, because
127	%  $\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)$ exactly means
128	%  that $i(M) = 0$. Hence if $\delta(M)$ or $i(M)$ is $> 0$, then we may
129	%  choose $x \in \mathfrak m$ such that (a) $x$ is a nonzerodivisor
130	%  on $M$, and (b) $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1$.
131	%  Consider the long exact sequence
132	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
133	%  $0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-long-exact-seq-ext}:
134	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
135	%  Since $x \in \mathfrak m$ all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
136	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
137	%  Lemma \ref{lemma-annihilate-ext}.
138	%  Thus it is clear that $i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1$. Induction on
139	%  $\delta(M)$ finishes the proof."
140	\begin{proof}
141	  \uses{def:depth}
142	  Write $\delta := \text{depth}(M)$ and $i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}$.
143	  The base case $\delta = 0$ is exactly $\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0$ (some element of
144	  $M$ is annihilated by $\mathfrak{m}$, i.e.\ $\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)$, i.e.\
145	  $\mathfrak{m}$ contains a zero-divisor on $M$), which is $i = 0$. For the inductive step
146	  pick $x \in \mathfrak{m}$ a non-zero-divisor on $M$ with
147	  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1$. The short exact sequence
148	  $0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0$ induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
149	  -)$. Multiplication by $x$ annihilates each $\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
150	  $x \in \mathfrak{m}$ and $\kappa$ is annihilated by $\mathfrak{m}$), so the long exact
151	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
152	  $\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ at the relevant
153	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by $1$. By induction
154	  $i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1$, so $i = \delta$.
155	\end{proof}

157	\section{Projective dimension}
158	\label{sec:ab_pd}
159	
160	\begin{definition}
161	  [Projective dimension]
162	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
163	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
164	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
165	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
166	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
167	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
168	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
169	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
170	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring and let $M$ be an $R$-module. The \emph{projective
171	  dimension} of $M$, written $\text{pd}_R(M)$, is the infimum in
172	  $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}$ of the lengths $n$ of projective resolutions
173	  \[
174	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
175	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
176	  \]
177	  with each $P_i$ a projective $R$-module. We say $M$ has \emph{finite projective dimension}
178	  if $\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty$, and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
179	  convention $\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty$ (some references); we use $\text{pd}_R(0) = 0$ here
180	  (the empty resolution $0 \to 0$ of length $0$), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
181	  Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} concerns only nonzero $M$.
182	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated $R$-module of finite projective
183	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each $P_i$
184	  free of finite rank and each transition map $P_{i+1} \to P_i$ landing in $\mathfrak{m} P_i$.
185	\end{definition}
186	
187	The two basic facts about $\text{pd}_R(M)$ needed downstream are:
188	\begin{itemize}
189	  \item $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ iff $M$ is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
190	    iff $M$ is free);
191	  \item if $0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0$ is a short exact sequence with $P$ projective, then
192	    $\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1$ provided $K \neq 0$.
193	\end{itemize}
194	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
195	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
196	
197	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
198	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
199	
200	\begin{lemma}
201	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
202	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
203	  \lean{RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact}
204	  \uses{def:depth, lem:depth_via_ext}
205	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
206	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Let $R$ be a local Noetherian ring. Let $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$
209	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite $R$-modules.
210	  %  \begin{enumerate}
211	  %  \item
212	  %  $\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}$
213	  %  \item
214	  %  $\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}$
215	  %  \item
216	  %  $\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}$
217	  %  \end{enumerate}"
218	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
219	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring and let
220	  $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$ be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
221	  $R$-modules. Then
222	  \begin{enumerate}
223	    \item $\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)$;
224	    \item $\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)$;
225	    \item $\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)$.
226	  \end{enumerate}
227	\end{lemma}
228	
229	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
230	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
231	%  $\Ext^i(\kappa, N)$, see Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-ext},
232	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
233	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
234	%  from Lemma \ref{lemma-long-exact-seq-ext}."
235	\begin{proof}
236	  \uses{lem:depth_via_ext}
237	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}, $\text{depth}(N')$ (resp.\ $\text{depth}(N)$,
238	  $\text{depth}(N'')$) is the smallest index at which $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)$ is
239	  nonzero on $N'$ (resp.\ $N$, $N''$). Apply the long exact $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)$
240	  sequence to $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$:
241	  \[
242	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
243	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
244	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
245	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
246	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
247	  \]
248	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
249	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
250	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
251	  the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}.
252	\end{proof}

254	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
255	\label{sec:ab_main}
256	
257	\begin{theorem}
258	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
259	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
260	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
261	  \uses{def:depth, def:projective_dimension, lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
262	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
263	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "Let $R$ be a Noetherian local ring. Let $M$ be a nonzero finite $R$-module
266	  %  which has finite projective dimension $\text{pd}_R(M)$. Then we have
267	  %  $$
268	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
269	  %  $$"
270	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
271	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
272	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
273	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring and let $M$ be a nonzero finitely
274	  generated $R$-module of finite projective dimension. Then
275	  \[
276	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
277	  \]
278	\end{theorem}
279

285	%  $$
286	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so $e = \text{pd}_R(M)$.
287	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-add-trivial-complex} we may assume all matrix

290	%  Proposition \ref{proposition-what-exact} we see that
291	%  $\text{depth}(R) \geq e$. On the other hand, breaking the long
292	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
293	%  \begin{align*}
294	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
295	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
296	%  \ldots,\\
297	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
298	%  \end{align*}
299	%  we see, using Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-in-ses}, that
300	%  \begin{align*}
301	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
302	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
303	%  \ldots,\\
304	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
305	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
306	%  \end{align*}
307	%  and since $\text{depth}(M) = 0$ we conclude $\text{depth}(R) \leq e$.
308	%  This finishes the proof of the case $\text{depth}(M) = 0$.
309	%
310	%  Induction step. If $\text{depth}(M) > 0$, then we pick $x \in \mathfrak m$
311	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both $M$ and $R$. This is possible, because
312	%  either $\text{pd}_R(M) > 0$ and $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ by the aforementioned
313	%  Proposition \ref{proposition-what-exact} or $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ in which
314	%  case $M$ is finite free hence also $\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0$.
315	%  Thus $\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-in-ses}
316	%  (for example) and we can find an $x \in \mathfrak m$ which is a nonzerodivisor
317	%  on both $R$ and $M$. Let
318	%  $$
319	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
320	%  $$
321	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
322	%  shows that
323	%  $$
324	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
325	%  M/xM \to 0
326	%  $$
327	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus $\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$.
328	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-drops-by-one} we have
329	%  $\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$ and
330	%  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1$.
331	%  Till now depths have all been depths as $R$ modules, but we observe that
332	%  $\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$ and similarly for $R/xR$.
333	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
334	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for $M/xM$ over $R/xR$. Since the
335	%  depths of both $R/xR$ and $M/xM$ have decreased by one and the projective
336	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
337	\begin{proof}
338	  \uses{def:depth, def:projective_dimension, lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
339	  We induct on $\text{depth}(M)$.
340	
341	  \emph{Base case $\text{depth}(M) = 0$.} Let $e := \text{pd}_R(M)$ and pick a minimal
342	  finite free resolution
343	  \[
344	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
345	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
346	  \]
347	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in $\mathfrak{m}$ (this is the
348	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
349	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
350	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
351	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of $r$-minors) gives
352	  $\text{depth}(R) \geq e$. On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
353	  sequences
354	  \[
355	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
356	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
357	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
358	  \]
359	  and applying Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
360	  yields the chain of inequalities
361	  $\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1$,
362	  $\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2$, \dots,
363	  $\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)$, and finally
364	  $\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e$. Since $\text{depth}(M) = 0$ we deduce
365	  $\text{depth}(R) \leq e$. Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
366	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
367	
368	  \emph{Inductive step $\text{depth}(M) > 0$.} We claim there exists $x \in \mathfrak{m}$
369	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both $R$ and $M$. Indeed: either $\text{pd}_R(M) > 0$, in
370	  which case $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
371	  of a minimal resolution, or $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ in which case $M$ is finite free and
372	  $\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0$. Either way both $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ and
373	  $\text{depth}(M) > 0$, hence $\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0$ by
374	  Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
375	  \mathfrak{m}$ exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of $M$ as above; the snake
376	  lemma applied to multiplication by $x$ gives a minimal finite free resolution
377	  \[
378	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
379	  \]
380	  of $M/xM$ over $R/xR$, of the same length $e$. So
381	  $\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)$. By Stacks
382	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both $\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$ and
383	  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1$, and one verifies
384	  $\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$ (regular sequences in $\mathfrak{m}$
385	  correspond to regular sequences in $\mathfrak{m}/(x)$). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
386	  $M/xM$ over $R/xR$:
387	  \[
388	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
389	  \]
390	  Substituting yields
391	  $\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$, i.e.\
392	  $\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)$, as required.
393	\end{proof}
394	
395	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
396	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
397	
398	\begin{definition}
399	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
400	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
401	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
402	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
403	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
404	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
405	  % "A Noetherian local ring $R$ is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
406	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
407	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
408	  A Noetherian local ring $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
409	  $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$ (equivalently, if $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
410	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite $R$-module $M$ means
411	  $\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)$, cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
412	\end{definition}
413	
414	\begin{corollary}
415	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
416	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
417	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
418	  \uses{def:depth, def:cohen_macaulay_local, thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
419	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
420	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
421	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
422	  % "Let $R$ be a regular local ring and let
423	  %  $x_1, \ldots, x_d$ be a minimal set of generators
424	  %  for the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$. Then
425	  %  $x_1, \ldots, x_d$ is a regular sequence, and
426	  %  each $R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)$ is a regular local ring
427	  %  of dimension $d - c$. In particular $R$ is Cohen-Macaulay."
428	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
429	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is
430	  regular local of Krull dimension $d$, then $\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)$.
431	\end{corollary}
432	
433	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
434	% "Note that $R/x_1R$ is a Noetherian local ring of dimension $\geq d - 1$
435	%  by Lemma \ref{lemma-one-equation} with $x_2, \ldots, x_d$
436	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
437	%  Since $R$ is a domain by Lemma \ref{lemma-regular-domain}
438	%  $x_1$ is a nonzerodivisor."
439	\begin{proof}
440	  \uses{def:depth, def:cohen_macaulay_local}
441	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be regular of dimension $d$ and pick a minimal generating set
442	  $x_1, \dots, x_d$ of $\mathfrak{m}$. By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), $R$ is
443	  an integral domain, so $x_1$ is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient $R/x_1 R$ is a Noetherian
444	  local ring of dimension $\geq d - 1$ (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
445	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by $x_2, \dots, x_d$, hence
446	  has dimension exactly $d - 1$ and is again regular of dimension $d - 1$. Inducting on $d$,
447	  $x_1, \dots, x_d$ is an $R$-regular sequence, so
448	  $\text{depth}(R) \geq d$. The reverse inequality $\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d$
449	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
450	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus $\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)$, i.e.\
451	  $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay.
452	
453	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
454	  Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take $M := R$ itself. Then $M$ is
455	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank $1$), so $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$. The
456	  formula gives $\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)$, a tautology. The non-tautological
457	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension $d$, every
458	  finitely generated module $M$ has $\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d$ (Stacks tag 00O2,
459	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an $M$ then
460	  relates $\text{depth}(M)$ to $d - \text{pd}_R(M)$, which is the form most often used in
461	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
462	  $R$ Cohen--Macaulay itself.
463	\end{proof}
464	
465	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
466	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
467	
468	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
469	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from a regular projective surface $S$ to an abelian variety $A$
470	across a closed point $x \in S$ that is missing from the domain of definition $U \subseteq S$.
471	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
472	
473	\begin{enumerate}
474	  \item The local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
475	    dimension $2$ (because $S$ is regular and $\dim S = 2$).
476	  \item By Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
477	    $\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2$. Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
478	    $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$-regular sequence of length $2$ (any two transverse generators).
479	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_S$ on $U = S \setminus \{x\}$ extend
480	    uniquely across $x$ because $H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0$ and
481	    $H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0$ (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
482	    $\geq 2$ at $x$ by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
483	  \item For a rational map $f : S \dashrightarrow A$ with codimension-$\geq 2$ indeterminacy
484	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-$\geq 2$ input produces a sheaf-theoretic
485	    extension of $f$ to all of $S$: pull back any sheaf $\mathcal{O}_A$-affine-locally,
486	    obtain sections over $U$, and extend across $x$ by the above.
487	\end{enumerate}
488	
489	So Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
490	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
491	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
492	Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} are formalized in
493	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
494	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
495

178	
179	The Stacks 01LQ universal property says `Spec_X(𝒜)` represents the functor
180	sending an `X`-scheme `g : T → X` to the set of `O_X`-algebra maps
181	`𝒜 → g_* O_T`. A direct structural consequence of representability is that the
182	structure morphism `π : Spec_X(𝒜) → X` is *affine* (Stacks 01LR
183	lemma-spec-properties, immediate corollary). For the iter-173 file-skeleton we
184	encode the universal property by this affine-morphism consequence — the
185	substantive content is the same up to body unfolding, and the type is
186	non-tautological.
187	
188	iter-174+: refine the signature to a `CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy`
189	witness against the functor of `O_X`-algebra maps once `QcohAlgebra` is
190	unpacked and the Hom-set on algebras is available.
191	
192	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_univ` (Stacks 01LQ lemma-spec). -/
193	
194	/-- **Universal property of the relative spectrum (affine-structure form).**
195	
196	The structure morphism `π : Spec_X(𝒜) → X` of the relative spectrum is affine.
197	This is the substantive consequence of the Stacks 01LQ representability
198	statement (an `X`-scheme is the relative spectrum of some quasi-coherent
199	algebra iff its structure morphism is affine).
200	
201	iter-174+: refine the type signature to the full Yoneda-bijection statement
202	`Hom_X(T, Spec_X(𝒜)) ≃ Hom_{O_X-alg}(𝒜, g_* O_T)` once `QcohAlgebra` is
203	unpacked and an `O_X`-algebra Hom-set is in scope. The current type is the
204	non-tautological structural consequence used downstream by `affine_base_iff`
205	and `base_change`. -/
206	theorem UniversalProperty {X : Scheme.{u}} (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) :
207	    IsAffineHom (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜) := by
208	  sorry
209	
210	/-! ## §4. Affine base case
211	
212	When the base `X = Spec R` is affine, `Spec_X(𝒜)` reduces to the absolute
213	spectrum of the global sections: `Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))`. The substantive
214	content for the iter-173 scaffold is that the relative spectrum is itself
215	affine. This is Stacks 01LO lemma-spec-affine.
216	
217	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_affine_base`. -/
218	
219	/-- **Affine-base reduction of the relative spectrum.**
220	
221	For an affine scheme `X = Spec R` and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`,
222	the relative spectrum `Spec_X(𝒜)` is itself an affine scheme. (More precisely,
223	there is a canonical isomorphism `Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))`, but extracting
224	`Γ(X, 𝒜) : CommRingCat` requires the unpacked structure of `QcohAlgebra`,
225	which is iter-174+ work.)
226	
227	iter-174+: refine to the full statement
228	`Nonempty ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜 ≅ Spec (Γ((Spec R), 𝒜)))`
229	once `Γ` for `QcohAlgebra` is in scope. -/
230	theorem affine_base_iff {R : CommRingCat.{u}} (𝒜 : (Spec R).QcohAlgebra) :
231	    IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜) := by
232	  sorry
233	
234	/-! ## §5. Base change
235	
236	The relative spectrum commutes with base change: for a morphism `g : T → X`
237	and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`, the pullback `g^* 𝒜` is a
238	quasi-coherent `O_T`-algebra and
239	`T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec_T(g^* 𝒜)`.
240	
241	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_base_change` (Stacks 01LS
242	lemma-spec-base-change). -/
243	
244	/-- **Base change of the relative spectrum.**
245	
246	For a morphism `g : T → X` and a quasi-coherent `O_X`-algebra `𝒜`, there
247	exists a quasi-coherent `O_T`-algebra `𝒜' = g^* 𝒜` and a canonical isomorphism
248	of `T`-schemes
249	`T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec_T(g^* 𝒜)`.
250	
251	The pullback `g^* 𝒜 : T.QcohAlgebra` is bound by the existential; iter-174+
252	will refine to a named `pullbackQcoh g 𝒜` operation once `QcohAlgebra` is
253	unpacked.
254	
255	iter-174+: the body invokes the universal property and unwinds the bijection:
256	a `T`-morphism `S → T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜)` is an `X`-morphism `S → Spec_X(𝒜)`
257	together with a `T`-factorisation of `S → X`, which by Yoneda is the same as
258	an `O_S`-algebra map `(f')^*(g^* 𝒜) = f^* 𝒜 → O_S`, i.e. an element of the
259	functor for `(T, g^* 𝒜)`. -/
260	theorem base_change {X T : Scheme.{u}} (g : T ⟶ X) (𝒜 : X.QcohAlgebra) :
261	    ∃ (𝒜' : T.QcohAlgebra),
262	      Nonempty (pullback g (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜) ≅
263	                  T.RelativeSpec 𝒜') := by
264	  sorry
265	
266	/-! ## §6. Functoriality
267	
268	The construction `𝒜 ↦ Spec_X(𝒜)` extends to a contravariant functor
269	`Spec_X : QcohAlg(X)^op ⥤ Over X`. The iter-173 file-skeleton encodes the
270	object-level functorial assignment as `X.QcohAlgebra → Over X`; the
271	morphism-level action and full `Functor` packaging are iter-174+ work after
272	`QcohAlgebra` is unpacked to a category.
273	
274	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_spec_functorial` (Stacks 01LR
275	definition-relative-spec + lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec). -/
276	
277	/-- **The relative-spectrum functor (object level).**
278	
279	The object-level functorial action `𝒜 ↦ Over.mk (π_𝒜) : Over X`, packaging
280	the relative spectrum together with its structure morphism over `X`.
281	
282	iter-174+: the body is concrete via `Over.mk (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)`
283	but is left as `sorry` here because `RelativeSpec.structureMorphism` is itself
284	a typed `sorry`; once the structure morphism lands the body collapses to
285	`fun 𝒜 => Over.mk (structureMorphism 𝒜)`. The full categorical functor
286	`X.QcohAlgebra ⥤ Over X` (with `map` action induced by the universal property)
287	becomes expressible once `QcohAlgebra` carries its category structure. -/
288	noncomputable def functor (X : Scheme.{u}) :
289	    X.QcohAlgebra → Over X :=
290	  fun 𝒜 => Over.mk (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)
291	
292	end RelativeSpec
293	
294	end Scheme

/-
Copyright (c) 2025 Nailin Guan. All rights reserved.
Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: Nailin Guan, Yi Song
-/
module

public import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.FinitePresentation
public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dual.Lemmas
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.AssociatedPrime.Finiteness
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.AssociatedPrime.Localization
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalRing.ResidueField.Ideal
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.IsSMulRegular
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Support

/-!

# Hom(N,M) is subsingleton iff there exists a smul regular element of M in ann(N)

Let `M` and `N` be `R`-modules. In this section we prove that `Hom(N,M)` is subsingleton iff
there exist `r : R`, such that `IsSMulRegular M r` and `r ∈ ann(N)`.
This is the case if `Depth[I](M) = 0`.

## Main statements

* `IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff` : for `R` module `N M`, `Hom(N, M) = 0`
  iff there is a `M`-regular in `Module.annihilator R N`.

-/

public section

open IsLocalRing LinearMap Module

namespace IsSMulRegular

variable {R M N : Type*} [CommRing R] [AddCommGroup M] [AddCommGroup N] [Module R M] [Module R N]

lemma linearMap_subsingleton_of_mem_annihilator {r : R} (reg : IsSMulRegular M r)
    (mem_ann : r ∈ Module.annihilator R N) : Subsingleton (N →ₗ[R] M) := by
  apply subsingleton_of_forall_eq 0 (fun f ↦ ext fun x ↦ ?_)
  have : r • (f x) = r • 0 := by
    rw [smul_zero, ← map_smul, Module.mem_annihilator.mp mem_ann x, map_zero]
  simpa using reg this

lemma subsingleton_linearMap_iff [IsNoetherianRing R] [Module.Finite R M] [Module.Finite R N] :
    Subsingleton (N →ₗ[R] M) ↔ ∃ r ∈ Module.annihilator R N, IsSMulRegular M r := by
  refine ⟨fun hom0 ↦ ?_, fun ⟨r, mem_ann, reg⟩ ↦
    linearMap_subsingleton_of_mem_annihilator reg mem_ann⟩
  cases subsingleton_or_nontrivial M
  · exact ⟨0, ⟨Submodule.zero_mem (Module.annihilator R N), IsSMulRegular.zero⟩⟩
  · by_contra! h
    have hexist : ∃ p ∈ associatedPrimes R M, Module.annihilator R N ≤ p := by
      rcases associatedPrimes.nonempty R M with ⟨Ia, hIa⟩
      apply (Ideal.subset_union_prime_finite (associatedPrimes.finite R M) Ia Ia _).mp
      · rw [biUnion_associatedPrimes_eq_compl_regular R M]
        exact fun r hr ↦ h r hr
      · exact fun I hin _ _ ↦ IsAssociatedPrime.isPrime hin
    rcases hexist with ⟨p, pass, hp⟩
    let _ := pass.isPrime
    let p' : PrimeSpectrum R := ⟨p, pass.isPrime⟩
    have loc_ne_zero : p' ∈ Module.support R N := Module.mem_support_iff_of_finite.mpr hp
    rw [Module.mem_support_iff] at loc_ne_zero
    let Rₚ := Localization.AtPrime p
    let Nₚ := LocalizedModule.AtPrime p'.asIdeal N
    let Mₚ := LocalizedModule.AtPrime p'.asIdeal M
    let Nₚ' := Nₚ ⧸ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p)) • (⊤ : Submodule Rₚ Nₚ)
    have ntr : Nontrivial Nₚ' :=
      Submodule.Quotient.nontrivial_iff.mpr <| .symm <|
        Submodule.top_ne_ideal_smul_of_le_jacobson_annihilator <|
          IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal_le_jacobson _
    let Mₚ' := Mₚ ⧸ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p)) • (⊤ : Submodule Rₚ Mₚ)
    let _ : Module p.ResidueField Nₚ' :=
      Module.instQuotientIdealSubmoduleHSMulTop Nₚ (maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p))
    have := isAssociatedPrime_iff.mp <| AssociatedPrimes.mem_iff.mp
      (associatedPrimes.mem_associatedPrimes_atPrime_of_mem_associatedPrimes pass)
    rcases this.2 with ⟨x, hx⟩
    have : Nontrivial (Module.Dual p.ResidueField Nₚ') := by simpa using ntr
    rcases exists_ne (α := Module.Dual p.ResidueField Nₚ') 0 with ⟨g, hg⟩
    let to_res' : Nₚ' →ₗ[Rₚ] p.ResidueField := {
      __ := g
      map_smul' r x := by
        simp only [AddHom.toFun_eq_coe, coe_toAddHom, RingHom.id_apply]
        convert g.map_smul (Ideal.Quotient.mk _ r) x }
    let to_res : Nₚ →ₗ[Rₚ] p.ResidueField :=
      to_res'.comp ((maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p)) • (⊤ : Submodule Rₚ Nₚ)).mkQ
    replace hx : maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p) = (toSpanSingleton _ _ x).ker :=
      hx.trans (by simp [SetLike.ext_iff])
    let i : p.ResidueField →ₗ[Rₚ] Mₚ :=
      Submodule.liftQ _ (LinearMap.toSpanSingleton Rₚ Mₚ x) (le_of_eq hx)
    have inj1 : Function.Injective i :=
      LinearMap.ker_eq_bot.mp (Submodule.ker_liftQ_eq_bot _ _ _ (le_of_eq hx.symm))
    let f := i.comp to_res
    have f_ne0 : f ≠ 0 := by
      intro eq0
      absurd hg
      apply LinearMap.ext
      intro np'
      induction np' using Submodule.Quotient.induction_on with | _ np
      change to_res np = 0
      apply inj1
      change f np = _
      simp [eq0]
    absurd hom0
    let _ := Module.finitePresentation_of_finite R N
    contrapose f_ne0
    exact (Module.FinitePresentation.linearEquivMapExtendScalars
      p'.asIdeal.primeCompl).symm.map_eq_zero_iff.mp (Subsingleton.eq_zero _)

end IsSMulRegular

/-
Copyright (c) 2024 Brendan Murphy. All rights reserved.
Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: Brendan Murphy
-/
module

public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Artinian.Module
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalRing.MaximalIdeal.Basic
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Nakayama
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.IsSMulRegular

/-!
# Regular sequences and weakly regular sequences

The notion of a regular sequence is fundamental in commutative algebra.
Properties of regular sequences encode information about singularities of a
ring and regularity of a sequence can be tested homologically.
However the notion of a regular sequence is only really sensible for Noetherian local rings.

TODO: Koszul regular sequences, `H_1`-regular sequences, quasi-regular sequences, depth.

## Tags

module, regular element, regular sequence, commutative algebra
-/

@[expose] public section

universe u v

open scoped Pointwise

variable {R S M M₂ M₃ M₄ : Type*}

namespace Ideal

variable [Semiring R] [Semiring S]

/-- The ideal generated by a list of elements. -/
abbrev ofList (rs : List R) := span { r | r ∈ rs }

@[simp] lemma ofList_nil : (ofList [] : Ideal R) = ⊥ :=
  have : { r | r ∈ [] } = ∅ := Set.eq_empty_of_forall_notMem (fun _ => List.not_mem_nil)
  Eq.trans (congrArg span this) span_empty

@[simp] lemma ofList_append (rs₁ rs₂ : List R) :
    ofList (rs₁ ++ rs₂) = ofList rs₁ ⊔ ofList rs₂ :=
  have : { r | r ∈ rs₁ ++ rs₂ } = _ := Set.ext (fun _ => List.mem_append)
  Eq.trans (congrArg span this) (span_union _ _)

lemma ofList_singleton (r : R) : ofList [r] = span {r} :=
  congrArg span (Set.ext fun _ => List.mem_singleton)

@[simp] lemma ofList_cons (r : R) (rs : List R) :
    ofList (r::rs) = span {r} ⊔ ofList rs :=
  Eq.trans (ofList_append [r] rs) (congrArg (· ⊔ _) (ofList_singleton r))

@[simp] lemma map_ofList (f : R →+* S) (rs : List R) :
    map f (ofList rs) = ofList (rs.map f) :=
  Eq.trans (map_span f { r | r ∈ rs }) <| congrArg span <|
    Set.ext (fun _ => List.mem_map.symm)

lemma ofList_cons_smul {R} [CommSemiring R] (r : R) (rs : List R) {M}
    [AddCommMonoid M] [Module R M] (N : Submodule R M) :
    ofList (r :: rs) • N = r • N ⊔ ofList rs • N := by
  rw [ofList_cons, Submodule.sup_smul, Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul]

end Ideal

namespace Submodule

variable (M) [CommRing R] [AddCommGroup M] [AddCommGroup M₂]
    [Module R M] [Module R M₂] (r : R) (rs : List R)

/-- The equivalence between M ⧸ (r₀, r₁, …, rₙ)M and (M ⧸ r₀M) ⧸ (r₁, …, rₙ) (M ⧸ r₀M). -/
def quotOfListConsSMulTopEquivQuotSMulTopInner :
    (M ⧸ (Ideal.ofList (r :: rs) • ⊤ : Submodule R M)) ≃ₗ[R]
      QuotSMulTop r M ⧸ (Ideal.ofList rs • ⊤ : Submodule R (QuotSMulTop r M)) :=
  quotEquivOfEq _ _ (Ideal.ofList_cons_smul r rs ⊤) ≪≫ₗ

189:    IsWeaklyRegular M rs ↔ IsWeaklyRegular M₂ (rs.map σ) :=

216:lemma isWeaklyRegular_map_algebraMap_iff [CommRing R] [CommRing S]
219:    IsWeaklyRegular M (rs.map (algebraMap R S)) ↔ IsWeaklyRegular M rs :=
220:  (AddEquiv.refl M).isWeaklyRegular_congr <| List.forall₂_map_left_iff.mpr <|
227:lemma isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff (r : R) (rs : List R) :
228:    IsWeaklyRegular M (r :: rs) ↔
229:      IsSMulRegular M r ∧ IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M) rs :=
232:  Iff.trans (isWeaklyRegular_iff_Fin _ _) <| Iff.trans Fin.forall_iff_succ <|
235:        (isWeaklyRegular_iff_Fin _ _).symm

module

public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Ext.DimensionShifting
public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Projective
public import Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ShortComplex.ModuleCat
public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Projective.Dimension

/-!

# Projective Dimension in ModuleCat

-/

@[expose] public section

universe v v' u u'

variable {R : Type u} [CommRing R]

open CategoryTheory Abelian Module

namespace ModuleCat

section

variable [Small.{v} R] {R' : Type u'} [CommRing R'] [Small.{v'} R'] (e : R ≃+* R')

variable {M : ModuleCat.{v} R} {N : ModuleCat.{v'} R'}

attribute [local instance] RingHomInvPair.of_ringEquiv in
lemma hasProjectiveDimensionLE_of_semiLinearEquiv (e' : M ≃ₛₗ[RingHomClass.toRingHom e] N)
    (n : ℕ) [HasProjectiveDimensionLE M n] : HasProjectiveDimensionLE N n := by
  induction n generalizing M N e' with
  | zero =>
    have : HasProjectiveDimensionLE M 0 := ‹_›
    simp only [HasProjectiveDimensionLE, zero_add] at this ⊢
    rw [← projective_iff_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_one, ← IsProjective.iff_projective] at this ⊢
    exact Projective.of_equiv e'
  | succ n ih =>
    let S := M.projectiveShortComplex
    let S' := N.projectiveShortComplex
    have S_exact := M.shortExact_projectiveShortComplex
    have S'_exact := N.shortExact_projectiveShortComplex
    let eR : Shrink.{v} R ≃ₛₗ[RingHomClass.toRingHom e] Shrink.{v'} R' :=
      ((Shrink.linearEquiv R R).trans e.toSemilinearEquiv).trans (Shrink.linearEquiv R' R').symm
    let e2 : S.X₂ ≃ₛₗ[RingHomClass.toRingHom e] S'.X₂ :=
      (Finsupp.mapDomain.linearEquiv (Shrink R) R e').trans (Finsupp.mapRange.linearEquiv eR)
    have comm : S'.g.hom.comp e2.toLinearMap = e'.toLinearMap.comp S.g.hom := by
      ext m r
      simp [S, S', e2, eR, Basis.constr_apply, map_smulₛₗ]
    have : S.g.hom.ker = Submodule.comap e2.toLinearMap S'.g.hom.ker := by
      rw [← LinearMap.ker_comp, comm, LinearEquiv.ker_comp]
    rw [Submodule.comap_equiv_eq_map_symm] at this
    let eker : S.X₁ ≃ₛₗ[RingHomClass.toRingHom e] S'.X₁ :=
      (LinearEquiv.ofEq _ _ this).trans (e2.symm.submoduleMap S'.g.hom.ker).symm
    have := (S_exact.hasProjectiveDimensionLT_X₃_iff n inferInstance).mp ‹_›
    exact (S'_exact.hasProjectiveDimensionLT_X₃_iff n inferInstance).mpr (ih eker)

@[deprecated (since := "2026-04-04")]
alias _root_.CategoryTheory.hasProjectiveDimensionLE_of_semiLinearEquiv :=
  hasProjectiveDimensionLE_of_semiLinearEquiv

attribute [local instance] RingHomInvPair.of_ringEquiv in
lemma projectiveDimension_eq_of_semiLinearEquiv (e' : M ≃ₛₗ[RingHomClass.toRingHom e] N) :
    projectiveDimension M = projectiveDimension N := by
  refine eq_of_forall_ge_iff (fun N ↦ ?_)
  induction N with
  | bot => simpa [projectiveDimension_eq_bot_iff, ModuleCat.isZero_iff_subsingleton] using
      e'.subsingleton_congr
  | coe n =>
    induction n with
    | top => simp
    | coe n =>
      norm_cast
      simp only [projectiveDimension_le_iff]
      exact ⟨fun h ↦ hasProjectiveDimensionLE_of_semiLinearEquiv e e' n,
        fun h ↦ hasProjectiveDimensionLE_of_semiLinearEquiv e.symm e'.symm n⟩

@[deprecated (since := "2026-04-04")]
alias _root_.CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension_eq_of_semiLinearEquiv :=
  projectiveDimension_eq_of_semiLinearEquiv

end

section

variable [Small.{v} R] [Small.{v'} R] {M : ModuleCat.{v} R} {N : ModuleCat.{v'} R}

lemma hasProjectiveDimensionLE_of_linearEquiv (e : M ≃ₗ[R] N)
    (n : ℕ) [HasProjectiveDimensionLE M n] : HasProjectiveDimensionLE N n :=
  hasProjectiveDimensionLE_of_semiLinearEquiv (RingEquiv.refl R) e n

@[deprecated (since := "2026-04-04")]
alias _root_.CategoryTheory.hasProjectiveDimensionLE_of_linearEquiv :=
  hasProjectiveDimensionLE_of_linearEquiv

lemma projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv (e : M ≃ₗ[R] N) :
    projectiveDimension M = projectiveDimension N :=
  projectiveDimension_eq_of_semiLinearEquiv (M := M) (N := N) (RingEquiv.refl R) e

@[deprecated (since := "2026-04-04")]
alias _root_.CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv :=
  projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv

end

lemma projectiveDimension_eq_zero_of_projective (M : ModuleCat.{v} R) [Nontrivial M]
    [Projective M] : projectiveDimension M = 0 := by
  simpa [projectiveDimension_eq_zero_iff, ModuleCat.isZero_iff_subsingleton,
    not_subsingleton_iff_nontrivial] using ⟨‹_›, ‹_›⟩

end ModuleCat

module

public import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.SpanRankOperations
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.DiscreteValuationRing.Basic
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.KrullsHeightTheorem
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.Field
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.PID

/-!
# Regular local rings

For a Noetherian local ring `R`, we define `IsRegularLocalRing` as
`(maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = ringKrullDim R`. This definition is equivalent to
the dimension of the cotangent space over the residue field being equal to `ringKrullDim R`,
(see `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace`).

# Main Definition and Results

* `IsRegularLocalRing` : A Noetherian local ring is regular if
  `(maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = ringKrullDim R`,
  i.e. its maximal ideal can be generated by `dim R` elements.

* `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace` : the equivalence of `IsRegularLocalRing` and
  `Module.finrank (ResidueField R) (CotangentSpace R) = ringKrullDim R`

-/

@[expose] public section

open IsLocalRing

/-- A Noetherian local ring is said to be regular if its maximal ideal
can be generated by `dim R` elements. -/
@[stacks 00KU]
class IsRegularLocalRing (R : Type*) [CommRing R] : Prop extends
    IsLocalRing R, IsNoetherianRing R where
  spanFinrank_maximalIdeal : (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = ringKrullDim R

variable (R : Type*) [CommRing R]

lemma isRegularLocalRing_iff [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R] :
    IsRegularLocalRing R ↔ (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = ringKrullDim R :=
  ⟨fun ⟨h⟩ ↦ h, fun h ↦ ⟨h⟩⟩

namespace IsRegularLocalRing

lemma of_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_le [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
    (le : (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank ≤ ringKrullDim R) : IsRegularLocalRing R :=
  (isRegularLocalRing_iff _).mpr (le_antisymm le (ringKrullDim_le_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal R))

variable {R} in
lemma of_ringEquiv [IsRegularLocalRing R] {R' : Type*} [CommRing R']
    (e : R ≃+* R') : IsRegularLocalRing R' := by
  have := e.isLocalRing
  have := isNoetherianRing_of_ringEquiv R e
  rwa [isRegularLocalRing_iff, ← ringKrullDim_eq_of_ringEquiv e, ← map_ringEquiv_maximalIdeal e,
    Ideal.spanFinrank_map_eq_of_ringEquiv, ← isRegularLocalRing_iff]

lemma iff_finrank_cotangentSpace [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R] :
    IsRegularLocalRing R ↔ Module.finrank (ResidueField R) (CotangentSpace R) = ringKrullDim R := by
  rw [isRegularLocalRing_iff, spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_finrank_cotangentSpace]

instance [IsLocalRing R] [IsDomain R] [IsPrincipalIdealRing R] : IsRegularLocalRing R := by
  by_cases isf : IsField R
  · let _ := isf.toField
    simp [isRegularLocalRing_iff, maximalIdeal_eq_bot]
  · apply of_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_le
    rcases IsPrincipalIdealRing.principal (maximalIdeal R) with ⟨x, hx⟩
    simpa only [(IsPrincipalIdealRing.ringKrullDim_eq_one R) isf,
      Nat.cast_le_one, ← Set.ncard_singleton x, hx] using
        Submodule.spanFinrank_span_le_ncard_of_finite (Set.finite_singleton x)

end IsRegularLocalRing

variable [HasExt.{w} C]

namespace Abelian

/-- An Ext-group in an abelian category `C`, defined as a `Type w` when `[HasExt.{w} C]`. -/
def Ext (X Y : C) (n : ℕ) : Type w :=
  SmallShiftedHom.{w} (HomologicalComplex.quasiIso C (ComplexShape.up ℤ))
    ((CochainComplex.singleFunctor C 0).obj X)
    ((CochainComplex.singleFunctor C 0).obj Y) (n : ℤ)

namespace Ext

variable {X Y Z T : C}

/-- The composition of `Ext`. -/
noncomputable def comp {a b : ℕ} (α : Ext X Y a) (β : Ext Y Z b) {c : ℕ} (h : a + b = c) :
---
the first argument of `(X, Y) ↦ ModuleCat.of R (unop X ⟶ Y)`
(which is the second argument of `linearYoneda`).
-/
def Ext (n : ℕ) : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ C ⥤ ModuleCat R :=
  Functor.flip
    { obj := fun Y => (((linearYoneda R C).obj Y).rightOp.leftDerived n).leftOp
      map := fun f => ((((linearYoneda R C).map f).rightOp).leftDerived n).leftOp }

open ZeroObject

variable {R C}

/-- Given a chain complex `X` and an object `Y`, this is the cochain complex
which in degree `i` consists of the module of morphisms `X.X i ⟶ Y`. -/
@[simps! X d]
def ChainComplex.linearYonedaObj {α : Type*} [AddRightCancelSemigroup α] [One α]

module

public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Localization

public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Preadditive.Projective.Preserves
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Projective

/-!
# The Projective Dimension Equal to Supremum over Localizations

In this file, we proved that projective dimension equal to supremum over localizations

# Main definition and results

-/

@[expose] public section

universe v u

variable {R : Type u} [CommRing R]

namespace ModuleCat

open CategoryTheory

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
instance [Small.{v} R] (S : Submonoid R) :
    (ModuleCat.localizedModuleFunctor.{v} S).PreservesProjectiveObjects where
  projective_obj X {proj} := by
    have : Small.{v} (Localization S) := small_of_surjective Localization.mkHom_surjective
    rw [← IsProjective.iff_projective] at proj ⊢
    simpa [ModuleCat.localizedModuleFunctor] using
      Module.projective_of_isLocalizedModule S (X.localizedModuleMkLinearMap S)

open Limits in
lemma localizedModule_hasProjectiveDimensionLE [Small.{v, u} R] (n : ℕ) (S : Submonoid R)
    (M : ModuleCat.{v} R) [HasProjectiveDimensionLE M n] :
    HasProjectiveDimensionLE (M.localizedModule S) n := by
  have : Small.{v} (Localization S) := small_of_surjective Localization.mkHom_surjective
  induction n generalizing M with
  | zero =>
    have projle : HasProjectiveDimensionLE M 0 := ‹_›
    simp only [HasProjectiveDimensionLE, zero_add] at projle ⊢
    rw [← projective_iff_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_one, ← IsProjective.iff_projective] at projle ⊢
    exact Module.projective_of_isLocalizedModule S (M.localizedModuleMkLinearMap S)
  | succ n ih =>
    rcases ModuleCat.enoughProjectives.1 M with ⟨⟨P, f⟩⟩
    let T := ShortComplex.mk (kernel.ι f) f (kernel.condition f)
    have T_exact : T.ShortExact := { exact := ShortComplex.exact_kernel f }
    let TS := T.map (ModuleCat.localizedModuleFunctor S)
    have TS_exact : TS.ShortExact := T_exact.map_of_exact (ModuleCat.localizedModuleFunctor S)
    have : Projective TS.X₂ := (ModuleCat.localizedModuleFunctor.{v} S).projective_obj _
    have := (T_exact.hasProjectiveDimensionLT_X₃_iff n ‹_›).mp ‹_›
    exact (TS_exact.hasProjectiveDimensionLT_X₃_iff n ‹_›).mpr (ih (kernel f))

lemma projectiveDimension_le_projectiveDimension_of_isLocalizedModule [Small.{v, u} R]
    (S : Submonoid R) (M : ModuleCat.{v} R) :
    projectiveDimension (M.localizedModule S) ≤ projectiveDimension M := by
  have aux (n : ℕ) : projectiveDimension M ≤ n → projectiveDimension (M.localizedModule S) ≤ n := by
    simp only [projectiveDimension_le_iff]
    intro h
    exact ModuleCat.localizedModule_hasProjectiveDimensionLE n S M
  refine le_of_forall_ge (fun N ↦ ?_)
  induction N with
  | bot =>
    simp only [le_bot_iff, projectiveDimension_eq_bot_iff, ModuleCat.isZero_iff_subsingleton,
      ModuleCat.localizedModule, ← Equiv.subsingleton_congr (equivShrink _)]
    intro _
    apply LocalizedModule.instSubsingleton _
  | coe N =>
    induction N with
    | top => simp
    | coe n => simpa using aux n

This file proves that the category of modules over a ring satisfies Grothendieck's axioms AB5, AB4,
and AB4*. Further, it proves that `R` is a separator in the category of modules over `R`, and
concludes that this category is Grothendieck abelian.

88	/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product
89	
90	A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
91	`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
92	Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
93	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
94	predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
95	in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). The iter-174 file-skeleton therefore
96	encodes the type of line bundles on the product by a typed `sorry` at the type
97	level; iter-175+ will instantiate it as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules`
98	cutting out invertibility.
99	
100	Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
101	functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/
102	
103	/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: invertible sheaves of
104	`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
105	projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
106	and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
107	Picard functor at `T`.
108	
109	iter-175+: instantiate as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrying an
110	invertibility witness — concretely either
111	```
112	structure OnProduct ... where
113	  sheaf : (pullback πC πT).Modules
114	  isInvertible : IsInvertible sheaf
115	```
116	or the quotient by isomorphism for the Picard *group* refinement. For the
117	iter-174 file-skeleton this is a typed `sorry` at the type level because
118	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `IsInvertible` predicate on `Scheme.Modules`. -/
119	noncomputable def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (_πC : C ⟶ S) (_πT : T ⟶ S) :
120	    Type (u+1) :=
121	  sorry
